claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start, I need to ask you about the child (or children) you're claiming for.

**How old is your child?**

This will help me determine which eligibility path applies:
- Children under 16 generally qualify automatically (subject to other checks)
- Children aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children aged 20 or over don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age, or if you have multiple children, let me know about each of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have one child - Alex, who is 8 years old and lives with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information! Let me continue with the assessment for Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Since Alex is 8 years old, they fall into the under-16 category. Now I need to check a few more things.

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

This is typically straightforward for younger children like Alex - most 8-year-olds are not in paid work, so if Alex is not working for pay, the answer would be "yes" (working less than 24 hours per week).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - he's only 8! He's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's perfect! Since Alex is not working, let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now the next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

In other words, Alex is not receiving benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in his own name, correct?

For an 8-year-old in primary school, this would typically be "yes" - they're not receiving these benefits in their own right.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now the next check:

**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

Since Alex is only 8 years old and in primary school, I assume he's not in an apprenticeship. Can you confirm he's not in any apprenticeship program?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - he's only 8! He's just in primary school, nowhere near doing an apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely right! Let me continue with the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about **your residency status** (as the person claiming Child Benefit).

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

This is one of the key residency requirements. If you normally live in the UK, that's the most straightforward way to meet this requirement. 

Alternatively, you can also qualify if you:
- Have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
- Have pre-settled status and meet certain conditions (working, studying, etc.)
- Are a Crown servant living abroad

Do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirement. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to check about your responsibility for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You mentioned earlier that Alex lives with you at home in Manchester, so this means you meet the responsibility requirement by living with the child. 

Can you confirm that Alex lives with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example, if Alex has another parent or guardian, are they claiming Child Benefit for him? 

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. If someone else is claiming but you both agree that you should be the claimant instead, that can be arranged. If there's a dispute about who should claim, HMRC would need to decide.

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about Alex's living situation.

**Is Alex currently:**
- In hospital?
- In local authority care?
- In a foster care arrangement?
- Or living normally at home with you?

You mentioned Alex lives at home with you, so I assume he's living normally at home. Can you confirm that Alex is not currently in hospital, in care, or in a foster arrangement?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has been in local authority care for a bit, but he comes home to stay with me on the weekends.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying that - this is important information. Let me navigate to check the care arrangement rules.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. This is an important factor. When a child is in local authority care, there are specific rules about Child Benefit eligibility.

**Let me ask you a few questions about Alex's care arrangement:**

1. **How long has Alex been in local authority care?** (Has it been 8 weeks or less, or longer than 8 weeks?)

2. **How much time does Alex spend at home with you each week?** You mentioned weekends - does this add up to at least 24 hours per week that he spends at home with you?

The rules state that:
- Child Benefit can continue for up to 8 weeks when a child first goes into care
- OR if the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home, you may still be eligible
- You can also claim if the child spends 2 consecutive nights per week or 7+ days in a row at home

Can you tell me more about the timing and how much time Alex spends with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been in care for a couple of months now, but yes, he does come home every weekend - so that's definitely more than 24 hours a week with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's very helpful information. Since Alex spends more than 24 hours per week at home with you (coming home every weekend), you meet the exception to the care rules. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I have one final important question:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

Since Alex is in local authority care, the council may be covering costs for his care arrangement. If the council is paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance, this typically affects Child Benefit eligibility.

Is the local council paying for Alex's care/accommodation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, the local authority does pay for his care while he's there.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Let me process this final piece of information.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Decision

I've completed the assessment, and unfortunately, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex** at this time.

### Why you're not eligible:

While you meet most of the eligibility requirements:
- ✓ Alex is 8 years old (under 16)
- ✓ You live in the UK (Manchester) and meet residency requirements
- ✓ You have responsibility for Alex, who spends more than 24 hours per week at home with you
- ✓ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex

**However**, the key issue is that Alex is in local authority care and the council is paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. Under UK Child Benefit rules, you generally cannot receive Child Benefit when the local council is financially supporting the child's care arrangement.

### What this means:

Even though Alex comes home to stay with you every weekend (which is wonderful and shows your ongoing care and responsibility), the fact that the local authority is covering the costs of his care during the week means Child Benefit cannot be paid to you during this period.

### What you might consider:

- If Alex's care arrangement changes in the future and he returns home full-time (and the council stops paying for his accommodation/maintenance), you would likely become eligible for Child Benefit at that point
- You may want to speak with your social worker about what financial support might be available to you in your current situation

I'm sorry this isn't the outcome you were hoping for. If you have any questions or if Alex's circumstances change, please don't hesitate to reach out.

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and meets the basic age requirement (under 16). Alex is not in paid work, does not receive benefits in his own right, and is not in an apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) and meets residency requirements. The claimant lives with Alex and has responsibility for him, with Alex coming home every weekend for more than 24 hours per week. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. However, Alex is in local authority care and the local council is paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. Under UK Child Benefit rules, you generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance, which disqualifies the claim for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets most eligibility criteria including UK residency and responsibility for Alex (who spends 24+ hours per week at home). However, because Alex is in local authority care with the council paying for his accommodation/maintenance, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit under the rule that prevents claims when the local council is financially supporting the child's care arrangement.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR do they spend at least 24 hours a week at home?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 8 weeks in care. Exception if child spends 24+ hours/week at home. Can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights/week or 7+ days in a row at home.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.reason: Local council is paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance
response.guidance: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance. This applies to informal care arrangements and some fostering situations.

The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) and meets residency requirements. The claimant lives with Alex and has responsibility for him, with Alex coming home every weekend for more than 24 hours per week. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. However, Alex is in local authority care and the local council is paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. Under UK Child Benefit rules, you generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance, which disqualifies the claim for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets most eligibility criteria including UK residency and responsibility for Alex (who spends 24+ hours per week at home). However, because Alex is in local authority care with the council paying for his accommodation/maintenance, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit under the rule that prevents claims when the local council is financially supporting the child's care arrangement.
